county_metadata.parent_county: Unorganized territory (former Aleutian Islands Census Division)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Aleutians East Borough organized 1987 from unorganized territory. Covers the eastern Aleutian Islands and Alaska Peninsula communities including Sand Point, King Cove, Akutan, Cold Bay, and Nelson Lagoon. Predominantly Unangan (Aleut) population with Russian colonial heritage. Fishing and cannery industries have been the economic mainstay since the late 1800s.
county_metadata.research_tips: The Aleutians East Borough covers remote islands and communities along the eastern Aleutian chain. Unangan (Aleut) families predominate and their genealogical records are split across several repositories: Russian Orthodox Church parish registers at the Library of Congress (Alaskan Russian Church Archives microfilm) cover baptisms, marriages, and burials from the 1820s for villages like Sand Point and King Cove. The Alaska State Archives in Juneau holds territorial vital records (births and deaths from 1913) and court records from the Third Judicial District. Federal census enumerations for the Aleutian Islands begin in 1880 and are available on FamilySearch and Ancestry. WWII-era forced relocation records for Unangan people are at NARA (RG 126, Office of Indian Affairs) and the Aleutian Pribilof Islands Association in Anchorage. BLM GLO land patents are searchable online for homestead claims. The University of Alaska Fairbanks (UAF) Alaska and Polar Regions Collections holds oral history interviews and photographs from Aleutian communities.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Aleutians East area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

source_profiles.source_name: Aleutians East Territorial and State Vital Records -- Alaska State Archives / Bureau of Vital Statistics
source_profiles.repository: Alaska State Archives, Juneau / Alaska Bureau of Vital Statistics
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Territorial birth and death registrations for Aleutians East area from 1913; marriage records from territorial period; modern vital records from the Alaska Bureau of Vital Statistics. Pre-1913 vital events must be traced through church records, Russian Orthodox parish registers, and missionary records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Territorial records generally complete from 1913; earlier coverage varies greatly by community

source_profiles.source_name: Aleutians East Probate and Court Records -- Third Judicial District Superior Court
source_profiles.repository: Alaska Superior Court, Third Judicial District / Alaska State Archives, Juneau
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Superior Court handles probate (wills, estates, guardianships), adoption, and civil cases for Aleutians East. Third Judicial District records. Older territorial court files transferred to Alaska State Archives. Commissioner's court records from the territorial period may predate borough organization

source_profiles.source_name: Aleutians East Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Aleutians East area from 1880 to 1950; Alaska was first enumerated in the 1880 federal census. Coverage for remote villages is often incomplete or delayed in early decades. Alaska was a district/territory until statehood in 1959
source_profiles.survival_notes: Coverage varies; remote communities may not have been enumerated until 1920 or later

source_profiles.source_name: Aleutians East Church and Mission Records
source_profiles.repository: Library of Congress / Denominational archives / Alaska State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Russian Orthodox parish registers are among the earliest genealogical records for Aleutians East. The Alaskan Russian Church Archives at the Library of Congress (microfilm, 401 reels) contain baptisms, marriages, and burials. The Orthodox Church in America archives hold additional records. Essential for tracing Creole, Alaska Native, and Russian families in the colonial and early American periods

source_profiles.source_name: Aleutians East Bureau of Indian Affairs Records -- NARA Pacific Alaska Region
source_profiles.repository: NARA Pacific Alaska Region (Anchorage/Seattle)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA records for Alaska Native communities in Aleutians East area including census rolls, school enrollment records, allotment files, and agency correspondence. Also includes ANCSA (Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act, 1971) enrollment records at the BIA Alaska Regional Office. Essential for tracing Alaska Native family lines

source_profiles.source_name: Aleutians East Land Patents, Homesteads, and Mining Claims -- BLM GLO / Alaska DNR
source_profiles.repository: BLM General Land Office Records (online) / Alaska DNR Recorder's Office
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents and homestead entries for Aleutians East area searchable on BLM GLO Records website. Alaska DNR Recorder's Office holds recorded documents (deeds, mortgages) for the recording district covering Aleutians East. Mining claims (both lode and placer) at BLM Alaska State Office. Alaska was a public land state -- most land was federal domain

source_profiles.source_name: Aleutians East WWII Unangan Evacuation and Relocation Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / Aleutian Pribilof Islands Association, Anchorage
source_profiles.coverage_notes: WWII forced evacuation of Unangan (Aleut) people from the Aleutian and Pribilof Islands. Internment camp records, relocation documentation, property claims, and repatriation files at NARA (RG 126). The Aleutian Pribilof Islands Association in Anchorage holds community records and oral histories

county_metadata.parent_county: Unorganized territory (former Aleutian Islands Census Division)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Aleutians West Census Area covers the western Aleutian Islands including Unalaska/Dutch Harbor, Adak, Atka, and St. Paul and St. George (Pribilof Islands). Unorganized census area. Site of the oldest Russian-American Company settlements in Alaska (Unalaska from 1759). Dutch Harbor is one of the busiest fishing ports in the U.S. WWII battleground -- Japanese occupation of Attu and Kiska.
county_metadata.research_tips: Aleutians West Census Area encompasses the western Aleutian Islands including Unalaska/Dutch Harbor and Adak. Russian colonial records are the earliest genealogical sources, as the Russian-American Company established settlements at Unalaska from 1759. Russian Orthodox parish registers at the Library of Congress (microfilm) record Unangan and Creole families from the late 1700s. The Church of the Holy Ascension in Unalaska (1825) has records at the Orthodox Church in America archives. Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and Superior Court probate files. Federal census for Aleutian Islands from 1880. WWII records are significant: the Japanese bombed Dutch Harbor (1942) and occupied Attu and Kiska -- military records at NARA include casualty lists and Unangan internment/relocation documentation. NARA Pacific Alaska Region (Anchorage/Seattle) holds BIA records for Aleut communities. FamilySearch has indexed Alaska vital records and census records searchable by community name.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Aleutians West area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

county_metadata.parent_county: Unified municipality formed from Greater Anchorage Area Borough and City of Anchorage
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipality of Anchorage formed 1975 by merger of the Greater Anchorage Area Borough (1964) and City of Anchorage. Alaska's largest city, home to about 40% of the state's population. Founded as a tent city for Alaska Railroad construction workers in 1914. Major military presence (JBER). The 1964 Good Friday Earthquake (9.2 magnitude) caused extensive destruction in Anchorage.
county_metadata.research_tips: The Municipality of Anchorage is Alaska's largest city and most records-rich jurisdiction. The Anchorage Museum at Rasmuson Center holds photographs, maps, and manuscript collections documenting families from the railroad-building era (1914-1923) onward. Alaska State Archives in Juneau holds territorial vital records (births/deaths 1913+), but Anchorage-area records are also at the Superior Court (Third Judicial District) for probate, guardianship, and adoption files. The Municipality of Anchorage maintains property records from 1975 (unification). For earlier land records, check BLM GLO patents (online) and the Anchorage Recording District at the Alaska DNR Recorder's Office. Military records are extensive: Elmendorf AFB (now JBER) and Fort Richardson personnel records are at NARA St. Louis. The Anchorage Genealogical Society maintains surname files and published indexes. UAF Alaska and Polar Regions Collections and the Alaska State Library in Juneau hold newspapers, city directories (Anchorage from 1930s), and Matanuska Colony settler records. FamilySearch has indexed Alaska vital records. The 1964 Good Friday Earthquake records (damage assessments, relief applications) are at NARA and the Alaska State Archives.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Anchorage area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

source_profiles.repository: Alaska State Library, Juneau / UAF / Newspapers.com
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Anchorage Daily Times / Anchorage Daily News and other Anchorage area newspapers. Obituaries, legal notices, mining news, marriage announcements, and community events. Alaska State Library holds microfilm of territorial and early statehood newspapers. Chronicling America has some digitized Alaska papers

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Bethel Census Area is an unorganized census area covering the Yukon-Kuskokwim Delta in western Alaska. Named after the community of Bethel on the Kuskokwim River, a regional hub. Predominantly Yup'ik population. Moravian Church missionaries arrived in the 1880s. Accessible primarily by air and river; no road connection to the state highway system.
county_metadata.research_tips: Bethel Census Area covers the Yukon-Kuskokwim Delta, home to Yup'ik peoples. Genealogical records begin with Moravian Church mission records from the 1880s at the Moravian Archives in Bethlehem, PA (baptisms, marriages, burials, and community registers for Bethel and surrounding villages). Catholic mission records from Jesuit missions along the Kuskokwim River are at the Oregon Province Archives of the Society of Jesus at Gonzaga University, Spokane. Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and Second Judicial District court records. Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA) records at NARA Pacific Alaska Region include census rolls, school records, and allotment files for villages in the Bethel Agency. Federal census coverage begins 1880 but is incomplete for remote villages until 1920-1940. The Association of Village Council Presidents (AVCP) in Bethel may have community records. UAF oral history collections include Yup'ik elders' interviews valuable for family history. Alaska Native allotment records are at BLM Alaska.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Bethel area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Bristol Bay Borough organized 1962, one of the earliest boroughs in Alaska. Centered on Naknek and King Salmon on the north shore of Bristol Bay. Home to the world's largest sockeye salmon fishery. Mixed Alaska Native (Yup'ik, Dena'ina), Russian, and Scandinavian heritage. Katmai National Park and the 1912 Novarupta eruption are in the borough's eastern portion.
county_metadata.research_tips: Bristol Bay Borough centers on Naknek and the world's largest sockeye salmon fishery. Genealogical records reflect a mix of Alaska Native (Yup'ik and Dena'ina Athabascan), Russian, and Scandinavian heritage. Russian Orthodox Church records from Nushagak Mission (1832) are among the earliest and are available on Library of Congress microfilm. Catholic mission records for Bristol Bay villages are at Gonzaga University (Oregon Province Archives). Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and Third Judicial District court/probate files. Cannery employment records from Bristol Bay salmon canneries (1890s-1950s) document seasonal workers and are scattered among company archives -- check UAF and the Alaska State Library. BIA records at NARA include census rolls and school enrollment for Bristol Bay Agency communities. Federal census from 1880 though remote village coverage is inconsistent before 1920. BLM GLO land patents searchable online.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Bristol Bay area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Chugach Census Area created 2019 from the eastern portion of the former Valdez-Cordova Census Area. Covers Prince William Sound communities: Cordova, Valdez, Whittier, Chenega Bay, and Tatitlek. Home to Sugpiaq/Alutiiq and Eyak peoples. Copper River and Northwestern Railway connected Kennecott copper mines to Cordova (1911-1938). The 1964 earthquake devastated Valdez and Chenega.
county_metadata.research_tips: Chugach Census Area was created in 2019 from the former Valdez-Cordova Census Area and covers Prince William Sound communities including Cordova, Valdez, and Whittier plus Chenega and Tatitlek. For records before 2019, search under Valdez-Cordova Census Area. The Chugach Alaska Corporation and Chugach Heritage Foundation in Anchorage hold records related to Sugpiaq/Alutiiq Alaska Native families. Russian Orthodox parish registers for Nuchek and Tatitlek from the mid-1800s are at the Library of Congress. Alaska State Archives has territorial vital records from 1913 and Third Judicial District court files. The Valdez Museum and Cordova Historical Society hold local family histories, photographs, and mining-era records (Kennecott Copper). Copper River and Northwestern Railway records at UAF document workers and communities. The 1964 earthquake destroyed old Valdez and Chenega -- NARA holds disaster relief records. BLM GLO patents and Alaska Native allotment records are at BLM Alaska State Office.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Chugach area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Copper River Census Area created 2019 from the western portion of the former Valdez-Cordova Census Area. Covers the upper Copper River valley including Glennallen, Chitina, McCarthy, and Mentasta. Home to Ahtna Athabascan peoples. The Kennecott copper mines (1903-1938) and Copper River and Northwestern Railway were major economic drivers.
county_metadata.research_tips: Copper River Census Area was created in 2019 from the former Valdez-Cordova Census Area and covers the upper Copper River valley including Glennallen, Chitina, and McCarthy. For records before 2019, search under Valdez-Cordova Census Area. The area has Ahtna Athabascan communities with records at the BIA NARA Pacific Alaska Region and Ahtna, Inc. in Glennallen. Episcopal Church mission records for Copper River communities from the early 1900s may be at the Episcopal Diocese of Alaska archives. Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and Third Judicial District court records. The Kennecott copper mining era (1900-1938) generated significant employee and community records, some at UAF and the NPS Wrangell-St. Elias National Park archives. BLM GLO land patents are searchable online. Federal census coverage for the Copper River area begins in 1900. McCarthy-Kennecott Historical Museum has local records and photographs.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Copper River area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

source_profiles.source_name: Copper River Territorial and State Vital Records -- Alaska State Archives / Bureau of Vital Statistics
source_profiles.repository: Alaska State Archives, Juneau / Alaska Bureau of Vital Statistics
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Territorial birth and death registrations for Copper River area from 1913; marriage records from territorial period; modern vital records from the Alaska Bureau of Vital Statistics. Pre-1913 vital events must be traced through church records, Russian Orthodox parish registers, and missionary records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Territorial records generally complete from 1913; earlier coverage varies greatly by community

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Superior Court handles probate (wills, estates, guardianships), adoption, and civil cases for Copper River. Third Judicial District records. Older territorial court files transferred to Alaska State Archives. Commissioner's court records from the territorial period may predate borough organization

source_profiles.source_name: Copper River Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Copper River area from 1880 to 1950; Alaska was first enumerated in the 1880 federal census. Coverage for remote villages is often incomplete or delayed in early decades. Alaska was a district/territory until statehood in 1959
source_profiles.survival_notes: Coverage varies; remote communities may not have been enumerated until 1920 or later

source_profiles.source_name: Copper River Church and Mission Records
source_profiles.repository: Library of Congress / Denominational archives / Alaska State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Church records for Copper River area from various denominations. Russian Orthodox records (if any) at the Library of Congress. Episcopal, Catholic, and Protestant mission records at denominational archives. Church records are important for vital events before territorial registration in 1913

source_profiles.source_name: Copper River Bureau of Indian Affairs Records -- NARA Pacific Alaska Region
source_profiles.repository: NARA Pacific Alaska Region (Anchorage/Seattle)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA records for Alaska Native communities in Copper River area including census rolls, school enrollment records, allotment files, and agency correspondence. Also includes ANCSA (Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act, 1971) enrollment records at the BIA Alaska Regional Office. Essential for tracing Alaska Native family lines

source_profiles.source_name: Copper River Land Patents, Homesteads, and Mining Claims -- BLM GLO / Alaska DNR
source_profiles.repository: BLM General Land Office Records (online) / Alaska DNR Recorder's Office
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents and homestead entries for Copper River area searchable on BLM GLO Records website. Alaska DNR Recorder's Office holds recorded documents (deeds, mortgages) for the recording district covering Copper River. Mining claims (both lode and placer) at BLM Alaska State Office. Alaska was a public land state -- most land was federal domain

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Denali Borough organized 1990. Covers the area surrounding Denali (formerly Mt. McKinley) and Denali National Park. Communities include Healy, Anderson, Cantwell, and McKinley Park. The Alaska Railroad passes through the borough. Gold mining in the Kantishna Hills dates from 1905. Tourism related to Denali National Park (est. 1917 as Mt. McKinley National Park) is a major industry.
county_metadata.research_tips: Denali Borough covers the area around Denali National Park including Healy, Anderson, and Cantwell. Organized in 1990, historical records predate the borough. Alaska Railroad employment records (1914 onward) document families along the rail corridor and are at NARA Pacific Alaska Region and the Alaska Railroad Corporation in Anchorage. Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and Fourth Judicial District court records. Mining records from the Kantishna district (gold mining from 1905) are at BLM Alaska and UAF. NPS Denali National Park archives hold records of park employees and homesteaders from the 1920s. Federal census coverage for interior Alaska begins 1900 but is sparse until 1920-1940. BIA records for Athabascan communities along the Nenana River are at NARA. BLM GLO homestead patents are searchable online. The Denali Borough is among the newest boroughs and administrative records are limited -- for earlier records, check the Fourth Judicial District Superior Court in Fairbanks.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Denali area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Dillingham Census Area is an unorganized census area in southwestern Alaska. Named for the community of Dillingham at the head of Nushagak Bay. Predominantly Yup'ik and Dena'ina Athabascan population. Russian fur traders established Alexandrovski Redoubt (1818) near present-day Dillingham. Salmon fishing and processing have been the economic foundation since the 1880s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Dillingham Census Area covers Bristol Bay's northern shore and Nushagak River communities, home to Yup'ik and Dena'ina Athabascan peoples. The Moravian Church established missions in the Nushagak-Togiak area from the 1880s -- mission records (baptisms, marriages, burials) are at the Moravian Archives in Bethlehem, PA. Russian Orthodox records for the Nushagak area date to the 1830s and are at the Library of Congress (microfilm). Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and Third Judicial District court records. BIA records at NARA Pacific Alaska Region include census rolls, school records, and allotment files for Dillingham Agency communities. Salmon cannery records from the Nushagak area (1880s-1950s) document seasonal workers -- check UAF and the Alaska State Library. Federal census from 1880 but remote village coverage is limited before 1920. BLM GLO patents and Alaska Native allotment records at BLM Alaska. The Sam Fox Museum in Dillingham holds local historical photographs and records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Dillingham area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

source_profiles.source_name: Dillingham Church and Mission Records
source_profiles.repository: Library of Congress / Denominational archives / Alaska State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Russian Orthodox parish registers are among the earliest genealogical records for Dillingham. The Alaskan Russian Church Archives at the Library of Congress (microfilm, 401 reels) contain baptisms, marriages, and burials. The Orthodox Church in America archives hold additional records. Essential for tracing Creole, Alaska Native, and Russian families in the colonial and early American periods

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Fairbanks North Star Borough organized 1964. Alaska's second-largest city and the interior's commercial and educational center. Founded during the 1902 gold rush by E.T. Barnette. Home to the University of Alaska Fairbanks (est. 1917). Major military installations: Fort Wainwright and Eielson AFB. Trans-Alaska Pipeline terminus at nearby North Pole.
county_metadata.research_tips: Fairbanks North Star Borough is the interior's population center and records hub. The University of Alaska Fairbanks (UAF) Alaska and Polar Regions Collections is a premier research repository holding mining company records, personal papers, photographs, newspapers (Fairbanks Daily News-Miner from 1903), and oral histories. Alaska State Archives in Juneau holds vital records from 1913 and Fourth Judicial District court/probate files. The Fairbanks Recorder's Office (Alaska DNR) has land records for the Fairbanks Recording District. For gold rush era research (1902+), check mining claim records at BLM Alaska and NARA Pacific Alaska Region. Military records from Fort Wainwright (est. 1939, Ladd Army Airfield) and Eielson AFB are at NARA St. Louis. The Tanana-Yukon Historical Society maintains collections at Pioneer Park in Fairbanks. Federal census from 1910 for Fairbanks (town incorporated 1903). Episcopal mission records for interior Alaska are at the Diocese of Alaska archives. BLM GLO homestead patents searchable online. Fairbanks city directories from the 1910s at UAF and Alaska State Library.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Fairbanks North Star area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents
